title: Ngân Hàng Câu Hỏi Phỏng Vấn Thiết Kế Hệ Thống
subtitle: Bộ câu hỏi nâng cao về hệ thống phân tán và delivery cho phỏng vấn backend và platform.

questions.question: Vì sao dùng a message queue between services?
questions.intent: Checks whether you understand decoupling and asynchronous work.
options: It buffers work and decouples producers from consumers.
B. It removes all need for retries.
C. It guarantees zero bugs.
questions.correct_answer: It buffers work and decouples producers from consumers.
questions.explanation: Queues help các service handle spikes, perform background work, and fail more independently. They improve elasticity, but introduce ordering, retry, and observability concerns.
compare: Synchronous call: simpler flow, tighter coupling.
Queue: more resilient to spikes, but operationally more complex.
questions.question_en: Why use a message queue between services?
questions.question_vi: Vì sao dùng a message queue between services?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. It buffers work and decouples producers from consumers.
questions.correct_answer_vi: It buffers work and decouples producers from consumers.
questions.explanation_en: Queues help services handle spikes, perform background work, and fail more independently. They improve elasticity, but introduce ordering, retry, and observability concerns.
questions.explanation_vi: Queues help các service handle spikes, perform background work, and fail more independently. They improve elasticity, but introduce ordering, retry, and observability concerns.
options_en: A. It buffers work and decouples producers from consumers.
B. It removes all need for retries.
C. It guarantees zero bugs.
options_vi: It buffers work and decouples producers from consumers.
B. It removes all need for retries.
C. It guarantees zero bugs.
compare_en: Synchronous call: simpler flow, tighter coupling.
Queue: more resilient to spikes, but operationally more complex.
compare_vi: Synchronous call: simpler flow, tighter coupling.
Queue: more resilient to spikes, but operationally more complex.
questions.intent_en: Checks whether you understand decoupling and asynchronous work.
questions.intent_vi: Checks whether you understand decoupling and asynchronous work.

questions.question: Vì sao idempotency important for queue consumers?
questions.intent: Tests whether you understand at-least-once delivery realities.
options: Bởi vì messages may be delivered more than once.
B. Bởi vì các queue never retry.
C. Bởi vì idempotency improves UI design.
questions.correct_answer: Bởi vì messages may be delivered more than once.
questions.explanation: Many messaging systems prefer at-least-once delivery. Consumers therefore need deduplication or safe write patterns so replayed messages do not create duplicate business effects.
compare: Idempotent consumer: safe with retry and replays.
Non-idempotent consumer: duplicates are much harder to control.
questions.question_en: Why is idempotency important for queue consumers?
questions.question_vi: Vì sao idempotency important for queue consumers?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. Because messages may be delivered more than once.
questions.correct_answer_vi: Bởi vì messages may be delivered more than once.
questions.explanation_en: Many messaging systems prefer at-least-once delivery. Consumers therefore need deduplication or safe write patterns so replayed messages do not create duplicate business effects.
questions.explanation_vi: Many messaging systems prefer at-least-once delivery. Consumers therefore need deduplication or safe write patterns so replayed messages do not create duplicate business effects.
options_en: A. Because messages may be delivered more than once.
B. Because queues never retry.
C. Because idempotency improves UI design.
options_vi: Bởi vì messages may be delivered more than once.
B. Bởi vì các queue never retry.
C. Bởi vì idempotency improves UI design.
compare_en: Idempotent consumer: safe with retries and replays.
Non-idempotent consumer: duplicates are much harder to control.
compare_vi: Idempotent consumer: safe with retry and replays.
Non-idempotent consumer: duplicates are much harder to control.
questions.intent_en: Tests whether you understand at-least-once delivery realities.
questions.intent_vi: Tests whether you understand at-least-once delivery realities.

questions.question: Sự khác nhau giữa logs, metrics, và traces là gì?
questions.intent: Checks whether you can explain observability layers clearly.
options: Logs record events, metrics summarize numeric behavior, traces follow request flow across services.
B. Chúng là different words for the same output.
C. Only metrics matter in production.
questions.correct_answer: Logs record events, metrics summarize numeric behavior, traces follow request flow across services.
questions.explanation: Each signal answers different questions. Metrics are good for dashboards and alerts, log give detail, and traces show distributed request paths and latency breakdowns.
compare: Metrics: fast aggregate visibility.
Logs: rich detail.
Traces: cross-service request context.
questions.question_en: What is the difference between logs, metrics, and traces?
questions.question_vi: Sự khác nhau giữa logs, metrics, và traces là gì?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. Logs record events, metrics summarize numeric behavior, traces follow request flow across services.
questions.correct_answer_vi: Logs record events, metrics summarize numeric behavior, traces follow request flow across services.
questions.explanation_en: Each signal answers different questions. Metrics are good for dashboards and alerts, logs give detail, and traces show distributed request paths and latency breakdowns.
questions.explanation_vi: Each signal answers different questions. Metrics are good for dashboards and alerts, log give detail, and traces show distributed request paths and latency breakdowns.
options_en: A. Logs record events, metrics summarize numeric behavior, traces follow request flow across services.
B. They are different words for the same output.
C. Only metrics matter in production.
options_vi: Logs record events, metrics summarize numeric behavior, traces follow request flow across services.
B. Chúng là different words for the same output.
C. Only metrics matter in production.
compare_en: Metrics: fast aggregate visibility.
Logs: rich detail.
Traces: cross-service request context.
compare_vi: Metrics: fast aggregate visibility.
Logs: rich detail.
Traces: cross-service request context.
questions.intent_en: Checks whether you can explain observability layers clearly.
questions.intent_vi: Checks whether you can explain observability layers clearly.

questions.question: A circuit breaker giải quyết vấn đề gì?
questions.intent: Tests whether you know how to contain cascading failures.
options: It temporarily stops các lời gọi to a dependency đang lỗi to protect the system.
B. It encrypts the database.
C. It replaces monitoring.
questions.correct_answer: It temporarily stops các lời gọi to a dependency đang lỗi to protect the system.
questions.explanation: If a downstream service is failing or slow, a circuit breaker can thất bại sớm instead of piling up more các request and consuming resources. Nó giới hạn cascading damage and gives các dependency time to recover.
compare: Nếu không có breaker: retry and waiting can amplify outages.
Với breaker: faster failure and better resource control.
questions.question_en: What problem does a circuit breaker solve?
questions.question_vi: A circuit breaker giải quyết vấn đề gì?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. It temporarily stops calls to a failing dependency to protect the system.
questions.correct_answer_vi: It temporarily stops các lời gọi to a dependency đang lỗi to protect the system.
questions.explanation_en: If a downstream service is failing or slow, a circuit breaker can fail fast instead of piling up more requests and consuming resources. It limits cascading damage and gives dependencies time to recover.
questions.explanation_vi: If a downstream service is failing or slow, a circuit breaker can thất bại sớm instead of piling up more các request and consuming resources. Nó giới hạn cascading damage and gives các dependency time to recover.
options_en: A. It temporarily stops calls to a failing dependency to protect the system.
B. It encrypts the database.
C. It replaces monitoring.
options_vi: It temporarily stops các lời gọi to a dependency đang lỗi to protect the system.
B. It encrypts the database.
C. It replaces monitoring.
compare_en: Without breaker: retries and waiting can amplify outages.
With breaker: faster failure and better resource control.
compare_vi: Nếu không có breaker: retry and waiting can amplify outages.
Với breaker: faster failure and better resource control.
questions.intent_en: Tests whether you know how to contain cascading failures.
questions.intent_vi: Tests whether you know how to contain cascading failures.

questions.question: Vì sao health checks useful in deployed services?
questions.intent: Checks whether you think about runtime lifecycle and traffic routing.
options: They help orchestrators know whether an instance is alive and ready to receive traffic.
B. They compile code automatically.
C. They remove the need for tests.
questions.correct_answer: They help orchestrators know whether an instance is alive and ready to receive traffic.
questions.explanation: Liveness and readiness checks let the platform restart broken instances and avoid sending traffic to instances that have started but are not yet ready to serve safely.
compare: Liveness: is the process still functioning.
Readiness: can it safely receive real traffic now.
questions.question_en: Why are health checks useful in deployed services?
questions.question_vi: Vì sao health checks useful in deployed services?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. They help orchestrators know whether an instance is alive and ready to receive traffic.
questions.correct_answer_vi: They help orchestrators know whether an instance is alive and ready to receive traffic.
questions.explanation_en: Liveness and readiness checks let the platform restart broken instances and avoid sending traffic to instances that have started but are not yet ready to serve safely.
questions.explanation_vi: Liveness and readiness checks let the platform restart broken instances and avoid sending traffic to instances that have started but are not yet ready to serve safely.
options_en: A. They help orchestrators know whether an instance is alive and ready to receive traffic.
B. They compile code automatically.
C. They remove the need for tests.
options_vi: They help orchestrators know whether an instance is alive and ready to receive traffic.
B. They compile code automatically.
C. They remove the need for tests.
compare_en: Liveness: is the process still functioning.
Readiness: can it safely receive real traffic now.
compare_vi: Liveness: is the process still functioning.
Readiness: can it safely receive real traffic now.
questions.intent_en: Checks whether you think about runtime lifecycle and traffic routing.
questions.intent_vi: Checks whether you think about runtime lifecycle and traffic routing.

questions.question: Trong trường hợp for a slow downstream dependency on a hot request path, cách nào thường an toàn hơn: unbounded waiting hay a timeout plus fallback/failure strategy?
options: Timeout plus fallback or controlled failure is usually safer.
B. Unbounded waiting is always better bởi vì các request should never fail fast.
C. System design ignores timeout behavior.
questions.correct_answer: Timeout plus fallback or controlled failure is usually safer.
questions.explanation: Infinite waiting ties up resources and can cascade instability through the whole system.
questions.code_snippet: result = client.get(timeout=2)\nif not result:\n    return cached_response()
compare: Unbounded wait: simpler but nguy hiểm under failure.
Bounded timeout strategy: safer under pressure.
questions.question_en: Which is usually safer for a slow downstream dependency on a hot request path: unbounded waiting or a timeout plus fallback/failure strategy?
questions.question_vi: Trong trường hợp for a slow downstream dependency on a hot request path, cách nào thường an toàn hơn: unbounded waiting hay a timeout plus fallback/failure strategy?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. Timeout plus fallback or controlled failure is usually safer.
questions.correct_answer_vi: Timeout plus fallback or controlled failure is usually safer.
questions.explanation_en: Infinite waiting ties up resources and can cascade instability through the whole system.
questions.explanation_vi: Infinite waiting ties up resources and can cascade instability through the whole system.
options_en: A. Timeout plus fallback or controlled failure is usually safer.
B. Unbounded waiting is always better because requests should never fail fast.
C. System design ignores timeout behavior.
options_vi: Timeout plus fallback or controlled failure is usually safer.
B. Unbounded waiting is always better bởi vì các request should never fail fast.
C. System design ignores timeout behavior.
compare_en: Unbounded wait: simpler but dangerous under failure.
Bounded timeout strategy: safer under pressure.
compare_vi: Unbounded wait: simpler but nguy hiểm under failure.
Bounded timeout strategy: safer under pressure.

questions.question: Trap in assuming autoscaling alone solves overload là gì?
options: Scaling is delayed and các dependency may still bottleneck or fail.
B. Autoscaling provides infinite instant capacity.
C. Overload only happens when code has syntax bugs.
questions.correct_answer: Scaling is delayed and các dependency may still bottleneck or fail.
questions.explanation: Autoscaling helps, but queue growth, cold starts, downstream limits, and cost ceilings still matter.
compare: Autoscaling as sole plan: optimistic and incomplete.
Autoscaling plus capacity planning: more realistic.
questions.question_en: What is the trap in assuming autoscaling alone solves overload?
questions.question_vi: Trap in assuming autoscaling alone solves overload là gì?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. Scaling is delayed and dependencies may still bottleneck or fail.
questions.correct_answer_vi: Scaling is delayed and các dependency may still bottleneck or fail.
questions.explanation_en: Autoscaling helps, but queue growth, cold starts, downstream limits, and cost ceilings still matter.
questions.explanation_vi: Autoscaling helps, but queue growth, cold starts, downstream limits, and cost ceilings still matter.
options_en: A. Scaling is delayed and dependencies may still bottleneck or fail.
B. Autoscaling provides infinite instant capacity.
C. Overload only happens when code has syntax bugs.
options_vi: Scaling is delayed and các dependency may still bottleneck or fail.
B. Autoscaling provides infinite instant capacity.
C. Overload only happens when code has syntax bugs.
compare_en: Autoscaling as sole plan: optimistic and incomplete.
Autoscaling plus capacity planning: more realistic.
compare_vi: Autoscaling as sole plan: optimistic and incomplete.
Autoscaling plus capacity planning: more realistic.

questions.question: Vì sao a dual write from database to message broker a classic trap?
options: One write may succeed while the other fails, causing divergence.
B. Databases and brokers commit atomically by default across all stacks.
C. Dual writes only matter in frontend apps.
questions.correct_answer: One write may succeed while the other fails, causing divergence.
questions.explanation: Điều này là why patterns like the outbox exist: to avoid inconsistent side effects across systems.
questions.code_snippet: save_order()\npublish_event()  # risky if second step fails
compare: Naive dual write: simple but inconsistent under failure.
Outbox pattern: more robust consistency handling.
questions.question_en: Why is a dual write from database to message broker a classic trap?
questions.question_vi: Vì sao a dual write from database to message broker a classic trap?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. One write may succeed while the other fails, causing divergence.
questions.correct_answer_vi: One write may succeed while the other fails, causing divergence.
questions.explanation_en: This is why patterns like the outbox exist: to avoid inconsistent side effects across systems.
questions.explanation_vi: Điều này là why patterns like the outbox exist: to avoid inconsistent side effects across systems.
options_en: A. One write may succeed while the other fails, causing divergence.
B. Databases and brokers commit atomically by default across all stacks.
C. Dual writes only matter in frontend apps.
options_vi: One write may succeed while the other fails, causing divergence.
B. Databases and brokers commit atomically by default across all stacks.
C. Dual writes only matter in frontend apps.
compare_en: Naive dual write: simple but inconsistent under failure.
Outbox pattern: more robust consistency handling.
compare_vi: Naive dual write: simple but inconsistent under failure.
Outbox pattern: more robust consistency handling.

questions.question: Vì sao relying on message ordering across unrelated partitions or consumers a trap?
options: Ordering guarantees are often limited in scope and can be broken by scaling choices.
B. All distributed các queue preserve one total global order automatically.
C. Ordering is irrelevant in event-driven systems.
questions.correct_answer: Ordering guarantees are often limited in scope and can be broken by scaling choices.
questions.explanation: Ordering semantics depend on the technology, partition key, and consumer model. Assumptions here frequently break in production.
questions.code_snippet: partition_key = user_id  # preserve per-user order, not global order
compare: Assumed global order: risky mental model.
Explicit scoped ordering: safer and more realistic.
questions.question_en: Why is relying on message ordering across unrelated partitions or consumers a trap?
questions.question_vi: Vì sao relying on message ordering across unrelated partitions or consumers a trap?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. Ordering guarantees are often limited in scope and can be broken by scaling choices.
questions.correct_answer_vi: Ordering guarantees are often limited in scope and can be broken by scaling choices.
questions.explanation_en: Ordering semantics depend on the technology, partition key, and consumer model. Assumptions here frequently break in production.
questions.explanation_vi: Ordering semantics depend on the technology, partition key, and consumer model. Assumptions here frequently break in production.
options_en: A. Ordering guarantees are often limited in scope and can be broken by scaling choices.
B. All distributed queues preserve one total global order automatically.
C. Ordering is irrelevant in event-driven systems.
options_vi: Ordering guarantees are often limited in scope and can be broken by scaling choices.
B. All distributed các queue preserve one total global order automatically.
C. Ordering is irrelevant in event-driven systems.
compare_en: Assumed global order: risky mental model.
Explicit scoped ordering: safer and more realistic.
compare_vi: Assumed global order: risky mental model.
Explicit scoped ordering: safer and more realistic.

questions.question: Vì sao độ trễ queue and tuổi xử lý valuable metrics in asynchronous systems?
options: They show how delayed work is becoming, not just how many messages exist.
B. They only matter for frontend rendering speed.
C. Message count alone always tells the full story.
questions.correct_answer: They show how delayed work is becoming, not just how many messages exist.
questions.explanation: Backlog age often reveals user-visible delay more directly than raw queue depth alone.
compare: Queue depth only: incomplete signal.
Lag/age metrics: closer to real delivery delay.
questions.question_en: Why are queue lag and processing age valuable metrics in asynchronous systems?
questions.question_vi: Vì sao độ trễ queue and tuổi xử lý valuable metrics in asynchronous systems?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. They show how delayed work is becoming, not just how many messages exist.
questions.correct_answer_vi: They show how delayed work is becoming, not just how many messages exist.
questions.explanation_en: Backlog age often reveals user-visible delay more directly than raw queue depth alone.
questions.explanation_vi: Backlog age often reveals user-visible delay more directly than raw queue depth alone.
options_en: A. They show how delayed work is becoming, not just how many messages exist.
B. They only matter for frontend rendering speed.
C. Message count alone always tells the full story.
options_vi: They show how delayed work is becoming, not just how many messages exist.
B. They only matter for frontend rendering speed.
C. Message count alone always tells the full story.
compare_en: Queue depth only: incomplete signal.
Lag/age metrics: closer to real delivery delay.
compare_vi: Queue depth only: incomplete signal.
Lag/age metrics: closer to real delivery delay.

questions.question: Trap in retrying immediately from every caller when a shared dependency fails là gì?
options: Immediate retry can amplify load and deepen the outage.
B. More retry always heal the dependency faster.
C. Shared các dependency are not affected by client retry behavior.
questions.correct_answer: Immediate retry can amplify load and deepen the outage.
questions.explanation: Backoff, jitter, and circuit breakers are used specifically bởi vì uncontrolled retry are nguy hiểm at scale.
compare: Immediate mass retry: outage amplifier.
Backoff and jitter: safer recovery posture.
questions.question_en: What is the trap in retrying immediately from every caller when a shared dependency fails?
questions.question_vi: Trap in retrying immediately from every caller when a shared dependency fails là gì?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. Immediate retries can amplify load and deepen the outage.
questions.correct_answer_vi: Immediate retry can amplify load and deepen the outage.
questions.explanation_en: Backoff, jitter, and circuit breakers are used specifically because uncontrolled retries are dangerous at scale.
questions.explanation_vi: Backoff, jitter, and circuit breakers are used specifically bởi vì uncontrolled retry are nguy hiểm at scale.
options_en: A. Immediate retries can amplify load and deepen the outage.
B. More retries always heal the dependency faster.
C. Shared dependencies are not affected by client retry behavior.
options_vi: Immediate retry can amplify load and deepen the outage.
B. More retry always heal the dependency faster.
C. Shared các dependency are not affected by client retry behavior.
compare_en: Immediate mass retry: outage amplifier.
Backoff and jitter: safer recovery posture.
compare_vi: Immediate mass retry: outage amplifier.
Backoff and jitter: safer recovery posture.

questions.question: Vì sao readiness check chỉ trả về trạng thái process đang chạy vẫn có thể gây hiểu lầm?
options: Process có thể vẫn còn sống nhưng chưa thể phục vụ an toàn do trạng thái khởi tạo hoặc trạng thái dependency.
B. Readiness and liveness are always identical by design.
C. Kubernetes ignores readiness endpoints anyway.
questions.correct_answer: Process có thể vẫn còn sống nhưng chưa thể phục vụ an toàn do trạng thái khởi tạo hoặc trạng thái dependency.
questions.explanation: Readiness check tốt phải phản ánh liệu instance có thực sự xử lý traffic đúng cách hay không, chứ không chỉ kiểm tra xem process có tồn tại hay không.
questions.code_snippet: if not db_ready or not cache_warm:\n    return 503
compare: Process-only check: too shallow.
Traffic-safety check: better readiness signal.
questions.question_en: Why can a readiness check that only returns process-up status still be misleading?
questions.question_vi: Vì sao readiness check chỉ trả về trạng thái process đang chạy vẫn có thể gây hiểu lầm?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. The process may be alive but unable to serve safely due to initialization or dependency state.
questions.correct_answer_vi: Process có thể vẫn còn sống nhưng chưa thể phục vụ an toàn do trạng thái khởi tạo hoặc trạng thái dependency.
questions.explanation_en: Good readiness checks reflect whether the instance can actually handle traffic correctly, not just whether a process exists.
questions.explanation_vi: Readiness check tốt phải phản ánh liệu instance có thực sự xử lý traffic đúng cách hay không, chứ không chỉ kiểm tra xem process có tồn tại hay không.
options_en: A. The process may be alive but unable to serve safely due to initialization or dependency state.
B. Readiness and liveness are always identical by design.
C. Kubernetes ignores readiness endpoints anyway.
options_vi: Process có thể vẫn còn sống nhưng chưa thể phục vụ an toàn do trạng thái khởi tạo hoặc trạng thái dependency.
B. Readiness and liveness are always identical by design.
C. Kubernetes ignores readiness endpoints anyway.
compare_en: Process-only check: too shallow.
Traffic-safety check: better readiness signal.
compare_vi: Process-only check: too shallow.
Traffic-safety check: better readiness signal.

questions.question: Which is a stronger first step for a slow distributed workflow: rewriting code blindly or mapping the latency budget across components?
options: Map the latency budget and identify where time is actually spent.
B. Rewrite the code blindly bởi vì all slow systems are caused by syntax.
C. Increase logging verbosity and stop there.
questions.correct_answer: Map the latency budget and identify where time is actually spent.
questions.explanation: Slow systems are often bottlenecked by storage, networks, serialization, queueing, or downstream behavior rather than one local code block.
compare: Measured latency budget: evidence-driven optimization.
Blind rewrite: expensive and low-confidence.
questions.question_en: Which is a stronger first step for a slow distributed workflow: rewriting code blindly or mapping the latency budget across components?
questions.question_vi: Which is a stronger first step for a slow distributed workflow: rewriting code blindly or mapping the latency budget across components?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. Map the latency budget and identify where time is actually spent.
questions.correct_answer_vi: Map the latency budget and identify where time is actually spent.
questions.explanation_en: Slow systems are often bottlenecked by storage, networks, serialization, queueing, or downstream behavior rather than one local code block.
questions.explanation_vi: Slow systems are often bottlenecked by storage, networks, serialization, queueing, or downstream behavior rather than one local code block.
options_en: A. Map the latency budget and identify where time is actually spent.
B. Rewrite the code blindly because all slow systems are caused by syntax.
C. Increase logging verbosity and stop there.
options_vi: Map the latency budget and identify where time is actually spent.
B. Rewrite the code blindly bởi vì all slow systems are caused by syntax.
C. Increase logging verbosity and stop there.
compare_en: Measured latency budget: evidence-driven optimization.
Blind rewrite: expensive and low-confidence.
compare_vi: Measured latency budget: evidence-driven optimization.
Blind rewrite: expensive and low-confidence.

questions.question: Vì sao 'make everything async' sometimes a trap in system design interviews?
options: Async introduces coordination, consistency, and observability costs that may not be justified everywhere.
B. Async eliminates every distributed-systems problem automatically.
C. Sync systems are forbidden in production.
questions.correct_answer: Async introduces coordination, consistency, and observability costs that may not be justified everywhere.
questions.explanation: Asynchrony is powerful, but it is not free. Good answers justify where it helps and where synchronous simplicity is better.
questions.code_snippet: sync for checkout response, async for email and analytics
compare: Everything async: fashionable but often overcomplicated.
Selective async: tradeoff-aware design.
questions.question_en: Why is 'make everything async' sometimes a trap in system design interviews?
questions.question_vi: Vì sao 'make everything async' sometimes a trap in system design interviews?
questions.correct_answer_en: A. Async introduces coordination, consistency, and observability costs that may not be justified everywhere.
questions.correct_answer_vi: Async introduces coordination, consistency, and observability costs that may not be justified everywhere.
questions.explanation_en: Asynchrony is powerful, but it is not free. Good answers justify where it helps and where synchronous simplicity is better.
questions.explanation_vi: Asynchrony is powerful, but it is not free. Good answers justify where it helps and where synchronous simplicity is better.
options_en: A. Async introduces coordination, consistency, and observability costs that may not be justified everywhere.
B. Async eliminates every distributed-systems problem automatically.
C. Sync systems are forbidden in production.
options_vi: Async introduces coordination, consistency, and observability costs that may not be justified everywhere.
B. Async eliminates every distributed-systems problem automatically.
C. Sync systems are forbidden in production.
compare_en: Everything async: fashionable but often overcomplicated.
Selective async: tradeoff-aware design.
compare_vi: Everything async: fashionable but often overcomplicated.
Selective async: tradeoff-aware design.

subtitle_en: Advanced distributed systems and delivery questions for backend and platform interviews.
